summary: Jeff Atwood recommends the O'Reilly book 'Designing Interfaces' which compiles UI patterns and techniques into a full-color reference. While acknowledging that the same information exists freely online across various UI pattern websites, he argues that the physical book format better serves visual design content. The post draws a parallel between code design patterns and UI design patterns, noting a crucial difference in their visibility to end users.
key_points: UI design patterns deserve the same systematic treatment as code design patterns
Physical books can be superior to digital for highly visual reference material
Several free online resources exist for GUI and UI pattern education
Unlike code patterns hidden in implementation, UI patterns are directly visible to users
money_quotes: Sometimes atoms are better than bytes.
Unlike any of the Design Patterns in that famous book, these patterns are visible to your users. Plan accordingly.
A Design Patterns for the eye instead of the mind.
key_insight: UI design patterns differ fundamentally from code patterns because users directly experience them, making visual design literacy essential for developers.
